title: Lamp Wells
desc: Quiet reading chambers need light tended as carefully as the books.

steps.text:
The reading chambers of Castle Pillar are quieter than the moving halls around them have any right to be. Narrow shafts catch outside light when they can. Mirrored bowls carry it deeper. In the dimmer corners, blue lamp cups and trimmed wick channels take over, holding enough brightness on the page without turning the room harsh.

A lamp hand meets you in the first chamber with a polishing cloth over one shoulder and a tray of fresh light cups in both hands. Readers are already settling in at the long stone tables, spreading notes and scrolls under a hush so complete that even boots seem to enter it respectfully.

"Light first," the lamp hand whispers, because somehow whispering feels required. The mirror bowls need polishing, or the blue cups and wick lines need replacing before the deeper alcoves go dim.

options.label: Polish the mirror bowls that feed the shafts
Replace the light cups and trim the wick channels

steps.text:
The mirror bowls sit under narrow slits in the stone, each one angled to carry a slice of daylight farther into the chamber. Dust dulls them quickly, not because Castle Pillar is dirty, but because old stone and constant motion leave a film on everything that stays still long enough.

You wipe each bowl slowly, careful not to disturb the angles. One by one the reflected light sharpens and lengthens, spreading across tabletops and shelf spines in thin clean bands. A reader in the far corner turns a page and finally stops leaning in so close to the text. Another lifts their head as if noticing the chamber itself for the first time all morning.

By the end of the row, the whole room seems calmer simply because it can be seen more clearly. The lamp hand glances across the restored light and smiles very slightly. "Good. Scholars become difficult when the letters disappear before the ideas do."

options.label: Fold the cloth and move to the next table

steps.text:
The blue cups are set into niches farther back where daylight arrives weak and tired. Some burn oil with mineral salts. Others hold slow-glowing fungus paste sealed under thin lids. Either way, each source has to be trimmed or replaced with a hand steady enough not to spill light where it does not belong.

You move from niche to niche with the lamp hand, lifting old cups, clearing the wick channels, and setting new light in place. The effect is immediate but gentle. A dark corner becomes readable. A carved catalog panel comes back into view. The edge of a stair no longer disappears into attractive gloom.

When the last alcove is relit, the chamber holds several kinds of quiet at once: thinking quiet, turning-page quiet, and the mechanical quiet of a place whose larger motions are being restrained just beyond the walls. Castle Pillar may be famous for puzzles and secrets, but this room reminds you it is also famous because people manage to study here at all.

options.label: Leave the chamber fit for reading
